editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to practise IELTS Speaking. Can you give me a Part 1-style question about “music” and then a Part 2 cue card about “a memorable trip”?

Sure. Part 1 (Music): “What kind of music do people in your country usually listen to, and why?”

Part 2 (Memorable trip): “Describe a trip you remember well. You should say: where you went, who you went with, what you did there, and explain why it was memorable for you.”
Can you add two follow-up Part 3 questions connected to travel and technology?

Of course. Part 3:
1) “How has smartphone technology changed the way people plan and experience travel?”
2) “Do you think virtual reality tours could replace some types of travel in the future? Why or why not?”
Also, I need a natural linking phrase instead of “On the other hand” for contrasting ideas.

You can use: “That said, …” or “Having said that, …” or “Still, …” depending on how strong the contrast is.

Example: “Many people prefer booking online. That said, some still trust travel agents for complex trips.”
Quick question: what’s the difference between a “direct flight” and a “non-stop flight”?

A “non-stop flight” goes from origin to destination without any stops. A “direct flight” keeps the same flight number but may include a stop to pick up or drop off passengers; you usually stay on the same aircraft, but not always.
For practice, I usually time myself because in the real Speaking test I panic if I go beyond one minute, so I keep checking the word limit while answering.

That’s a smart routine. If you’re aiming for 80–110 words, you can plan a quick structure: 1 short opening sentence, 2–3 supporting sentences, and a brief wrap-up. To avoid going over time, try speaking at a steady pace and stop after your final point, even if you feel you could add more. Also, record yourself once or twice and notice how many seconds 100 words takes for you—then you’ll internalize the timing without constantly counting.

Actually, I think I said it wrong before. During Nowruz, I don’t really enjoy travelling in those 13 days, because everywhere is crowded and expensive. I usually stay in my city and just do short visits.

So you still enjoy the holiday, but the part you *don’t* like is travelling during the 13-day break, mainly due to the crowds and higher costs, and you prefer staying local and keeping it simple with brief family visits.
Yes, exactly. What I enjoy most is spending time with my family and relatives, and watching the special Nowruz TV programmes at home, not travelling.
